content.text: A self-driving vehicle's sensors can see perhaps 150 metres ahead. V2X communication can extend that awareness to hundreds of metres around corners, through buildings, and seconds into the future — by letting the vehicle receive information from other vehicles, traffic infrastructure, pedestrian devices, and network services that have already perceived what the vehicle cannot yet see. V2X is the difference between a vehicle that reacts to events and one that anticipates them.
Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) is an umbrella term for wireless communication between a vehicle and any entity in its operating environment. The sub-categories are:
direct peer-to-peer exchange of position, speed, heading, and intent between vehicles, enabling cooperative collision avoidance and platooning

communication with roadside units (RSUs), traffic signals, work zone warnings, and variable message signs; most consequentially, Signal Phase and Timing (SPAT) messages that tell a vehicle exactly when the light will turn green
communication with personal ITS devices (smartphones, wearables) carried by pedestrians and cyclists, enabling beyond-sensor awareness of vulnerable road users
communication with cloud services via cellular networks, used for HD map updates, fleet management, and high-latency but globally-sourced information (hazard alerts, routing)
Two competing radio access technologies carry these messages:
based on IEEE 802.11p operating in the 5.9 GHz WAVE (Wireless Access in Vehicular Environments) band, and
defined by 3GPP in Release 14 (LTE-V) and Release 16 (NR-V2X), which runs over both direct sidelink (PC5 interface, no base station required) and network paths (Uu interface via 5G).

Road fatalities are dominated by situations where a driver cannot see or react in time: a vehicle running a red light, a child stepping from between parked cars, a truck jackknifing on an icy motorway around a blind curve. V2X addresses exactly these scenarios. The US NHTSA estimated in 2014 that V2V technology alone could prevent or mitigate 70–80% of multi-vehicle crashes involving unimpaired drivers — a figure roughly equivalent to eliminating drunk driving entirely.

For autonomous vehicles the value proposition is different but equally compelling. AVs rely on onboard sensors (LIDAR, radar, cameras) that have finite range and line-of-sight requirements. V2X extends the effective perception horizon, allows cooperative manoeuvre planning between AVs that share intent, and provides access to infrastructure data (signal timing, road geometry at intersections) that onboard sensors cannot infer reliably. It is, in effect, a global distributed sensor network that any connected vehicle can read.
Cooperative perception: receive object lists from nearby vehicles, extending awareness beyond sensor range and around occlusions
SPAT integration: smooth approach to intersections using precise green-phase timing, reducing hard braking and fuel consumption
Early hazard warning: receive DENM (Decentralized Environmental Notification Message) alerts for accidents, road works, or emergency vehicles before they are visible
Cooperative manoeuvre planning: V2V negotiation of merge, lane-change, and platooning manoeuvres reduces uncertainty and enables tighter following distances
V2P safety: detect pedestrians and cyclists in blind spots or behind vehicles through their personal device broadcasts
Reduced sensor requirements: shared perception can decrease the sensor suite needed per vehicle in connected environments
Infrastructure-to-vehicle geometry: MAP messages provide precise intersection geometry that supplements or corrects HD map data
DSRC and C-V2X are incompatible at the radio layer; mixed deployments require dual-radio or software-defined radio hardware, and no consensus standard has been settled globally
V2I requires roadside infrastructure deployment by government or operators; V2V benefits require critical mass of equipped vehicles — both are expensive chicken-and-egg problems
cellular paths (V2N) have variable latency under load; safety-critical applications must use direct sidelink (V2V) or DSRC for time-sensitive messages
authenticated broadcast messages expose vehicle positions; pseudonym certificates (changing frequently) are required to prevent tracking while maintaining trust
the 5.9 GHz band shared with DSRC is increasingly crowded with WiFi (RLAN) in many jurisdictions, degrading range and reliability
a compromised or malfunctioning vehicle can broadcast false data (ghost vehicles, incorrect SPAT state) — receivers must validate messages against physical plausibility

The message layer for V2X is defined by SAE J2735 (North America) and ETSI ITS (Europe, increasingly adopted globally). Both define a common set of message types. The two most important for autonomous vehicles are:
BSM / CAM (Basic Safety Message / Cooperative Awareness Message):

broadcast by every equipped vehicle at 1–10 Hz. Contains: station ID (pseudonymous), timestamp, position (WGS-84, centimetre accuracy), speed, heading, acceleration, vehicle dimensions, and brake status. Recipients fuse incoming BSMs into a local dynamic map (LDM) to track all nearby equipped vehicles.

broadcast by traffic signal controllers via RSUs. SPAT (Signal Phase And Timing) gives the current and upcoming signal states for each movement group at the intersection, with timing estimates (minimum, maximum, and most-likely end time for each phase). MAP gives the intersection geometry — lane topology, stop bar positions, and movement connections. Together they allow a vehicle to compute a Target Speed Advisory (TSA): the speed at which to approach the intersection to arrive during green without stopping.

At the radio layer, DSRC uses 802.11p with 10 MHz channels in the 5.9 GHz band. The Control Channel (CCH, 5.90 GHz) carries safety messages; Service Channels carry other data. 802.11p removes the association handshake of normal WiFi, enabling broadcasts to reach any receiver in range (up to ~300 m for vehicles, ~1 km from elevated RSUs) with latency under 10 ms.

C-V2X PC5 (direct sidelink) uses LTE-D2D or NR sidelink, also in the 5.9 GHz ITS band. It offers better range (~500 m), better non-line-of-sight performance, and tighter synchronization compared to 802.11p — at the cost of higher hardware complexity. 5G NR-V2X (Release 16) adds unicast and groupcast sidelink modes, enabling reliable vehicle-to-vehicle data streams for cooperative driving (platooning, remote teleoperation).

attrs.alt: V2X communication architecture showing Vehicle AV-001 and AV-002 exchanging V2V messages, connecting to an RSU traffic signal via V2I SPAT messages, a pedestrian V2P device, a 5G tower for V2N, and a cloud backend, with latency budget and standards summary panels

content.text: The diagram shows the four V2X communication modes and their typical latency targets. V2V via DSRC or C-V2X PC5 achieves under 10 ms round-trip between vehicles at highway spacing. V2I SPAT messages are broadcast continuously from the RSU; the vehicle receives them passively (no round-trip required) with delivery latency under 20 ms. V2P messages follow the same broadcast model. V2N over 5G carries higher-latency, globally-sourced data (HD map tiles, fleet telemetry) where sub-50 ms latency is acceptable. The latency hierarchy directly determines which messages can support safety-critical functions.
Urban AV deployment at signalized intersections — SPAT/MAP integration is one of the highest-ROI V2X use cases, with measurable fuel and brake wear reductions
Highway platooning — V2V enables tight convoy spacing (< 10 m headway) through real-time speed and brake state sharing between platoon members
Intersection collision avoidance — V2V BSM fusion detects conflicting movements from vehicles approaching cross streets before either vehicle can see the other
Work zone management — V2I DENM alerts notify approaching vehicles of temporary speed limits and lane closures beyond sensor range
Emergency vehicle pre-emption — V2I signals from emergency vehicles to traffic controllers and approaching civilian vehicles enable safer clearance corridors
Vulnerable road user protection — V2P integration with smartphone apps allows pedestrians to alert nearby vehicles of their intent to cross
Connected fleet operations — V2N provides fleet-level telemetry aggregation, remote software updates, and dynamic routing based on real-time traffic data
Start with V2I SPAT integration, which has defined standards and available RSU infrastructure in many cities. Obtain a J2735 UPER decoder (open-source options include

library with the J2735 schema). Subscribe to SPAT messages from RSUs in your test corridor (most smart city deployments expose them via UDP multicast on the DSRC channel or via an API gateway). Parse the signal state and timing fields; implement the Target Speed Advisory computation.

For V2V, deploy an OBU (On-Board Unit) with a DSRC or C-V2X chipset (Qualcomm 9150 C-V2X, NXP SAF5400 DSRC). Generate BSMs at 10 Hz with your vehicle's GPS position, speed, and heading. Receive neighbor BSMs and fuse into your local dynamic map alongside your onboard sensor detections. The most important fusion step is coordinate frame alignment — all BSMs are in WGS-84; your sensor objects are in vehicle-local coordinates; accurate ego-position (RTK GPS, < 30 cm) is required for safe fusion.

"""
V2I Signal Phase and Timing (SPAT) integration example.
Demonstrates receiving SPAT/MAP messages from a Roadside Unit (RSU)
and using phase timing to plan a smooth approach to a signalized intersection.

Standards references:
  - SAE J2735: Dedicated Short Range Communications (DSRC) Message Set Dictionary
  - ETSI EN 302 637-2: Cooperative Awareness Message (CAM)
  - IEEE 1609.3: WAVE (Wireless Access in Vehicular Environments)
"""

import time
import math
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional, List
from enum import Enum


class SignalState(Enum):
    UNAVAILABLE = 0
    DARK = 1
    STOP_THEN_PROCEED = 2   # flashing red
    STOP_AND_REMAIN = 3     # red
    PRE_MOVEMENT = 4        # red-yellow (some countries)
    PERMISSIVE_MOVEMENT = 5 # green with yield
    PROTECTED_MOVEMENT = 6  # green (protected turn)
    PERMISSIVE_CLEARANCE = 7 # yellow with yield
    PROTECTED_CLEARANCE = 8  # yellow (protected)
    CAUTION_CONFLICTING = 9  # flashing yellow


@dataclass
class MovementEvent:
    """Single phase event from a SPAT movement state list."""
    signal_state: SignalState
    min_end_time: float         # seconds from now (lower bound)
    max_end_time: float         # seconds from now (upper bound)
    likely_end_time: float      # best estimate


@dataclass
class SPATMessage:
    """Parsed subset of SAE J2735 SPAT message."""
    intersection_id: int
    timestamp_ms: int           # message generation time (TAI ms)
    movement_events: List[MovementEvent]

    @property
    def age_seconds(self) -> float:
        """How old is this SPAT message (based on wall clock)."""
        return (time.time_ns() // 1_000_000 - self.timestamp_ms) / 1000.0

    def current_phase(self) -> Optional[MovementEvent]:
        """Return the currently active movement event."""
        if not self.movement_events:
            return None
        return self.movement_events[0]


class SPATDecoder:
    """
    Minimal SAE J2735 SPAT decoder (UPER-encoded ASN.1).
    In production use python-asn1 or a compiled C extension (libj2735).
    This example operates on a pre-parsed dict for clarity.
    """

    @staticmethod
    def from_dict(raw: dict) -> SPATMessage:
        events = []
        for ev in raw.get("movementList", []):
            state = ev.get("movementEvent", {})
            events.append(MovementEvent(
                signal_state=SignalState(state.get("eventState", 0)),
                min_end_time=state.get("timing", {}).get("minEndTime", 0) / 10.0,
                max_end_time=state.get("timing", {}).get("maxEndTime", 0) / 10.0,
                likely_end_time=state.get("timing", {}).get("likelyTime", 0) / 10.0,
            ))
        return SPATMessage(
            intersection_id=raw["intersectionID"],
            timestamp_ms=raw["timeStamp"],
            movement_events=events,
        )


class GreenWaveAdvisor:
    """
    Compute an advisory speed for an approaching vehicle to catch the green phase.

    If the vehicle arrives during red, it suggests a target arrival time (start of
    next green) and computes the required speed to reach the stop bar exactly then.
    If it can arrive during green, it confirms nominal speed is acceptable.
    """

    def __init__(self, max_decel: float = 2.0, max_accel: float = 1.5):
        self.max_decel = max_decel   # m/s²
        self.max_accel = max_accel   # m/s²

    def advise(
        self,
        spat: SPATMessage,
        distance_to_stopbar: float,    # metres
        current_speed: float,          # m/s
    ) -> dict:
        """
        Returns a dict with:
          advised_speed: target speed in m/s
          action: "maintain" | "slow" | "accelerate" | "stop"
          eta_seconds: estimated time to stop bar at advised speed
          reason: human-readable explanation
        """
        if spat.age_seconds > 2.0:
            return {"action": "stop", "reason": "SPAT message stale (>2s)", "advised_speed": 0}

        phase = spat.current_phase()
        if phase is None:
            return {"action": "stop", "reason": "No phase data", "advised_speed": 0}

        eta_nominal = distance_to_stopbar / max(current_speed, 0.1)

        if phase.signal_state in (SignalState.PROTECTED_MOVEMENT,
                                   SignalState.PERMISSIVE_MOVEMENT):
            # Green phase — check we fit within remaining green time
            if eta_nominal <= phase.likely_end_time:
                return {
                    "action": "maintain",
                    "advised_speed": current_speed,
                    "eta_seconds": eta_nominal,
                    "reason": f"Will arrive in {eta_nominal:.1f}s, green ends ~{phase.likely_end_time:.1f}s",
                }
            else:
                # Try to accelerate to catch the green
                # distance = v*t => v_needed = d / green_end_time
                v_needed = distance_to_stopbar / phase.likely_end_time
                if v_needed <= current_speed + self.max_accel * phase.likely_end_time:
                    return {
                        "action": "accelerate",
                        "advised_speed": round(v_needed, 1),
                        "eta_seconds": phase.likely_end_time,
                        "reason": f"Accelerate to {v_needed:.1f}m/s to catch green",
                    }

        # Red or can't catch green — target start of next green phase
        if phase.signal_state in (SignalState.STOP_AND_REMAIN,):
            # Assume next green starts approximately after max_end_time
            time_to_green = phase.max_end_time
            v_target = distance_to_stopbar / max(time_to_green, 1.0)
            return {
                "action": "slow",
                "advised_speed": round(max(v_target, 0), 1),
                "eta_seconds": time_to_green,
                "reason": f"Red until ~{time_to_green:.0f}s; approach at {v_target:.1f}m/s",
            }

        return {"action": "stop", "reason": "Unknown phase", "advised_speed": 0}


# --- Simulation ---
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Simulate receiving a SPAT message from RSU at intersection 1042
    raw_spat = {
        "intersectionID": 1042,
        "timeStamp": int(time.time() * 1000) - 200,   # 200ms ago
        "movementList": [{
            "movementEvent": {
                "eventState": 3,     # STOP_AND_REMAIN (red)
                "timing": {
                    "minEndTime": 120,    # 12.0 seconds until phase change
                    "maxEndTime": 145,    # 14.5 seconds upper bound
                    "likelyTime": 130,    # 13.0 seconds most likely
                }
            }
        }]
    }

    spat = SPATDecoder.from_dict(raw_spat)
    advisor = GreenWaveAdvisor()

    advice = advisor.advise(
        spat,
        distance_to_stopbar=85.0,   # 85 metres away
        current_speed=13.9,         # ~50 km/h
    )
    print("V2I Advisory:", advice)
    # Expected: slow to ~6.5 m/s to arrive as green begins
Cooperative Awareness Message (CAM) generation and fusion into a Local Dynamic Map:

"""
Cooperative Awareness Message (CAM) generation and processing.
ETSI EN 302 637-2 (CAM v1.4.1).

CAMs are broadcast at 1–10 Hz by every ITS station (vehicle, cyclist, pedestrian)
and contain position, heading, speed, and acceleration. Recipients fuse incoming
CAMs into a local dynamic object map to extend beyond-sensor awareness.
"""

import time
import math
import struct
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Dict, Optional

@dataclass
class ITSStationBasicContainer:
    station_type: int       # 5 = passenger car, 15 = pedestrian, 10 = motorcycle
    reference_position: tuple   # (lat_1e7, lon_1e7, alt_cm)

@dataclass
class HighFrequencyContainer:
    heading: float          # degrees from north (0-359.9)
    speed: float            # m/s
    longitudinal_accel: float   # m/s², forward positive
    lateral_accel: float
    yaw_rate: float         # deg/s
    drive_direction: int    # 0=forward, 1=backward, 2=unavailable

@dataclass
class CAM:
    """Simplified ETSI EN 302 637-2 CAM."""
    station_id: int
    generation_time_ms: int     # TAI milliseconds
    basic: ITSStationBasicContainer
    hf: HighFrequencyContainer

    def age_ms(self) -> int:
        return int(time.time() * 1000) - self.generation_time_ms

    def position_wgs84(self):
        lat, lon, alt = self.basic.reference_position
        return lat / 1e7, lon / 1e7, alt / 100.0

    def is_stale(self, max_age_ms: int = 1500) -> bool:
        return self.age_ms() > max_age_ms


class LocalDynamicMap:
    """
    Fuse incoming CAMs into a local object list for cooperative perception.
    Vehicles share their CAMs; the receiving vehicle merges them with its
    own sensor data to build an extended situational awareness picture.
    """

    def __init__(self, stale_threshold_ms: int = 1500):
        self._objects: Dict[int, CAM] = {}   # station_id → latest CAM
        self.stale_threshold_ms = stale_threshold_ms

    def update(self, cam: CAM):
        self._objects[cam.station_id] = cam

    def active_objects(self) -> List[CAM]:
        now = int(time.time() * 1000)
        return [
            cam for cam in self._objects.values()
            if not cam.is_stale(self.stale_threshold_ms)
        ]

    def nearby_vehicles(self, ego_lat: float, ego_lon: float,
                        radius_m: float = 200.0) -> List[CAM]:
        """Return all active ITS stations within radius_m metres of ego."""
        result = []
        for cam in self.active_objects():
            lat, lon, _ = cam.position_wgs84()
            d = haversine_m(ego_lat, ego_lon, lat, lon)
            if d <= radius_m:
                result.append(cam)
        return result

    def collision_risk_objects(self, ego_lat: float, ego_lon: float,
                               ego_speed: float, horizon_s: float = 5.0) -> List[tuple]:
        """
        Simple TTC (time-to-collision) screening.
        Returns list of (cam, ttc_seconds) for objects with TTC < horizon_s.
        """
        risks = []
        for cam in self.nearby_vehicles(ego_lat, ego_lon):
            lat, lon, _ = cam.position_wgs84()
            dist_m = haversine_m(ego_lat, ego_lon, lat, lon)
            closing_speed = ego_speed - cam.hf.speed   # positive if closing
            if closing_speed > 0.5:
                ttc = dist_m / closing_speed
                if ttc < horizon_s:
                    risks.append((cam, round(ttc, 2)))
        return sorted(risks, key=lambda x: x[1])


def haversine_m(lat1: float, lon1: float, lat2: float, lon2: float) -> float:
    """Haversine great-circle distance in metres."""
    R = 6_371_000
    phi1, phi2 = math.radians(lat1), math.radians(lat2)
    dphi = math.radians(lat2 - lat1)
    dlam = math.radians(lon2 - lon1)
    a = math.sin(dphi/2)**2 + math.cos(phi1) * math.cos(phi2) * math.sin(dlam/2)**2
    return 2 * R * math.asin(math.sqrt(a))

The US Department of Transportation's Connected Vehicle Pilot Programme deployed V2I DSRC infrastructure at 10,000+ intersections and corridor miles across New York City, Tampa, and Wyoming. In NYC, over 8,000 taxis and transit vehicles were equipped with OBUs. SPAT data from RSUs enabled Transit Signal Priority (TSP) for buses — the bus broadcasts its identity and schedule delay; the RSU extends green phases to let it pass without stopping. Measured results showed 8–10% reduction in bus dwell time at equipped intersections.

In Europe, the C-Roads initiative operates coordinated V2X deployments across 16 countries under the ETSI ITS standard suite. The C-ITS Station standard (CEN/TS 17419) defines a conformance test suite that enables interoperability between vehicles and infrastructure from different manufacturers — a critical enabler for cross-border deployments. Germany's Autobahn motorway network began deploying C-V2X RSUs in 2022 for hazard warnings and roadworks notifications, with vehicles from BMW, Mercedes, and Volkswagen equipped with compatible OBUs.
Treating BSMs/CAMs as ground truth — they are self-reported and unverified; always apply physical plausibility checks (speed limits, heading consistency) before acting on them
Using V2N (cellular) for safety-critical functions — cellular latency is variable and can exceed 100 ms under congestion; use direct sidelink or DSRC for time-sensitive messages
Ignoring SPAT timing uncertainty — SPAT messages provide min/max/likely end times, not guaranteed phase change moments; advisors must account for the uncertainty range
Fusing V2V objects without accounting for sender GPS error — a vehicle reporting its position with 2 m GPS error produces an object with ±4 m combined position uncertainty
Broadcasting BSMs without pseudonym rotation — static station IDs allow vehicle tracking; implement pseudonym change at each key rotation interval per ETSI TS 102 941
Assuming the 5.9 GHz channel is exclusive — WiFi RLAN devices share the band in many regions; implement congestion control (DCC, Decentralized Congestion Control) as specified in ETSI EN 302 571
Implement ETSI ITS-G5 or IEEE WAVE stack correctly before custom extensions — the base standards define channel access, message timing, and security; deviating breaks interoperability
Use a dual-radio OBU (DSRC + C-V2X) during the standards transition period to maximize coverage and prepare for the eventual winner

Apply a consistency filter to incoming BSMs: reject objects whose reported speed exceeds road speed limits by more than 30%, or whose position jumps more than physically possible between consecutive messages
Stagger BSM generation time slightly from other vehicles (randomize within the 100 ms window) to reduce channel collision probability under high vehicle density
Design the SPAT advisory to be advisory, not mandatory — the vehicle should always be prepared to stop normally if the light is red on arrival regardless of what SPAT predicted
Log all received V2X messages with timestamps for post-incident analysis; message logs are invaluable for reconstructing the information available to the vehicle at the moment of an incident

DSRC channel capacity limits practical vehicle density. At 10 Hz per vehicle with a 300-byte BSM, the raw bitrate per vehicle is 24 kbps. The 10 MHz DSRC Control Channel supports roughly 200–300 vehicles at full 10 Hz broadcast rate before packet collision rates degrade reception. ETSI's Decentralized Congestion Control (DCC) standard reduces transmission rate as channel load increases — dropping to 1 Hz at high density to maintain acceptable packet delivery ratios.

5G NR-V2X (Release 16) significantly extends capacity through scheduled sidelink (mode 1, base-station managed) and NR HARQ retransmissions, enabling reliable unicast for platooning data at sub-1 ms latency over direct links. C-V2X PC5 Release 14/15 already outperforms DSRC in range and non-LOS environments in controlled testing, though real-world dense urban performance comparisons remain an active research area.
North American DSRC message set and performance requirements
UPER ASN.1 encoding; python-asn1 for decoding
DSRC + C-V2X dual-radio hardware for research
Linux-based; SDK available for custom applications
Used in BMW iX, Ford, GM C-V2X production vehicles
Software OBU stack with V2X security (PKI)
Supports ETSI ITS and SAE J2945; DSRC and C-V2X
V2X messages injected into CARLA / SUMO simulation

V2X security is built around a Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) for vehicles called a Credential Management System (SCMS in the US, C-ITS PKI in the EU). Each vehicle holds a set of pseudonymous certificates — short-lived public/private key pairs that change frequently (e.g., every 5 minutes) and carry no persistent identifier. All broadcast messages are signed with the current pseudonymous certificate. Recipients verify the signature against the certificate, and verify the certificate's chain of trust back to the root CA, before acting on the message.

The primary attack vector is message injection: a compromised vehicle or attacker with a software-defined radio can attempt to broadcast false BSMs, fake emergency vehicle alerts, or spoofed SPAT states. Mitigations include: (1) misbehaviour detection by SCMS, where recipients can report implausible messages and revoke the associated certificate; (2) corroboration with onboard sensors — a claimed ghost vehicle should appear in radar or camera if it is at the claimed range; (3) rate limiting and anomaly scoring on incoming V2X message feeds at the application layer.

V2X extends an autonomous vehicle's effective perception horizon beyond sensor range by sharing data from other vehicles (V2V), traffic infrastructure (V2I), pedestrian devices (V2P), and cloud services (V2N)
The two competing radio technologies are DSRC (IEEE 802.11p) and C-V2X (3GPP LTE-D / NR-V2X); both operate at 5.9 GHz with direct sidelink for safety-critical P2P messages requiring sub-10 ms latency

SPAT/MAP messages from traffic signal controllers enable Target Speed Advisory — the vehicle computes the speed at which to approach an intersection to arrive during green, reducing stops and fuel consumption

CAM/BSM messages broadcast at 10 Hz per vehicle carry position, speed, and heading; recipients fuse them into a Local Dynamic Map alongside onboard sensor detections, requiring accurate ego-position (< 30 cm) for safe object fusion

V2X security relies on a PKI-based Credential Management System with pseudonymous certificates that rotate every few minutes to enable message integrity verification without enabling long-term vehicle tracking

Misbehaviour detection — flagging physically implausible messages and revoking associated certificates — is essential because any equipped vehicle (or attacker) can inject signed messages into the shared broadcast channel

The deployment transition period requires dual-radio hardware (DSRC + C-V2X) or software-defined radio approaches; C-V2X NR-V2X (5G) is the expected long-term standard for safety-critical vehicle communication
